title: Advancing the Southwest Safety Net Embedded Scientist Research and Training (SSNE-STaR) Center

summary.hook: This poster describes SSNE-STaR, an AHRQ/PCORI-funded Learning Health System Center designed to build embedded CER/PCOR capacity within safety net organizations serving medically underserved populations.
summary.summary_30s: This poster describes SSNE-STaR, an AHRQ/PCORI-funded Learning Health System Center designed to build embedded CER/PCOR capacity within safety net organizations serving medically underserved populations. The program uses three cores (Administration, Research Education, Research Data Analysis) plus structured mentorship (ASU research mentor + clinical mentor) and “Rapid Implementers” to help scholars disseminate findings into practice. Early results report 18 enrolled scholars (16 active) embedded across Arizona safety net systems—exceeding the original 12-scholar target—suggesting strong demand and growing cross-institutional collaboration.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Equip embedded scholars to design, conduct, and disseminate/implement CER/PCOR projects within safety net systems.

Approach: Population: SSNE-STaR “Scholars” (embedded scientists/trainees) and their associated safety net clinical settings. Primary outcome: Not stated on poster (implied outcomes include enrollment, active embedding, and progress through project development pipeline).
Finding: Since launch, SSNE-STaR enrolled 18 scholars, with 16 currently active and embedded across Arizona safety net health systems.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: The SSNE-STaR Center addresses health disparities by training scholars, building infrastructure, and fostering equity-focused research; it is presented as a replicable model for improving healthcare in underserved populations. Implications (poster-grounded): A structured, mentored embedded scientist model can expand CER/PCOR capacity within safety net systems. Early enrollment exceeding target suggests strong demand and potential scalability. Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Program design uses three cores + dual-mentor model + rapid implementers to bridge research and practice. Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): The program is framed as an AHRQ/PCORI-funded Learning Health System Center and “one of 16” in the national program.
key_numbers: “Cohort: Enrollment Chart (2024–2026)” and “Scholar Research Development Pipeline (Aug 2024–Mar 2026)”.
18 scholars enrolled; 16 active and embedded across Arizona safety net systems.
Year-2 Rapid Implementers support dissemination and implementation.
Early results report 18 enrolled scholars (16 active) embedded across Arizona safety net systems—exceeding the original 12-scholar target—suggesting strong demand and growing cr...
takeaways: Since launch, SSNE-STaR enrolled 18 scholars, with 16 currently active and embedded across Arizona safety net health systems.
Enrollment exceeded the original 12-scholar target, suggesting strong demand for structured CER/PCOR training within clinical settings.
Scholars represent major partner organizations including Banner Health, Valleywise Health, District Medical Group, Phoenix Children’s Hospital, and Barrow Neurological Institute.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What are the eligibility criteria for becoming a Scholar, and how are scholars selected?

what_to_ask.question: The poster reports 18 enrolled and 16 active—what are the main reasons for non-active status (drops, role changes, time constraints)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: How do you evaluate whether SSNE-STaR increases CER/PCOR capacity—pre/post measures, competencies, productivity, or implementation outcomes?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: What are the practical benefits for safety net systems hosting scholars (short-term and long-term)?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.
